name: Buddhaprakash Patil

summary: 3+ years building payment and data platforms at Amdocs (T-Mobile project). I design microservices that stay correct under retries, concurrency and partial failure — idempotency, event-driven messaging, and resilient integrations are my day job.

caseStudies.title: PayFlow — correctness under concurrency and retries
caseStudies.context: Digital wallet backend (Java 21, Spring Boot 3, two microservices) built to production standards: the four failure modes payments systems face, each solved and tested.

decisions.problem: Two concurrent transfers corrupt a balance (lost update)
decisions.choice: Optimistic locking via @Version + bounded retry loop
decisions.why: Transfers on the same wallet are low-contention; optimistic avoids holding row locks. Under flash-sale contention I'd flip to SELECT ... FOR UPDATE — and the retry loop lives in a separate bean because self-invocation bypasses the Spring transaction proxy.

decisions.problem: Client retries a timed-out request → double spend
decisions.choice: Idempotency keys enforced by a DB unique constraint
decisions.why: An in-memory check loses the race between two concurrent duplicates; the constraint is the only arbiter that survives it. The stored response is replayed on retry.

decisions.problem: "Save to DB then publish event" crashes in between (dual write)
decisions.choice: Transactional outbox + polling relay, idempotent consumer
decisions.why: Outbox row commits atomically with the balance change; at-least-once delivery + eventId dedupe in the consumer composes into exactly-once processing — same recipe as Kafka with idempotent consumers.
Every transfer writes entries that net to zero; the invariant is asserted in tests, so corruption is detectable, auditable and attributable.

caseStudies.outcome: Concurrency test suite: 10 simultaneous transfers with exact money conservation; duplicate idempotency keys produce one debit; ledger always nets to zero.

caseStudies.title: Spring Batch ETL — Snowflake to PostgreSQL at scale
caseStudies.context: Amdocs (T-Mobile): recurring ingestion jobs moving analytics data from QuantumOS Snowflake into PostgreSQL, originally slow and failure-prone.

decisions.problem: Peak run times blowing past their window
decisions.choice: SQL query tuning, table partitioning, and parallel step execution
decisions.why: Profiling showed reads dominated; partitioning let independent slices run concurrently without contention on the write side.

decisions.problem: Ingestion failures mid-run leaving partial state
decisions.choice: Input validation up front + retry logic on transient failures
decisions.why: Rejecting malformed rows before the write phase turned whole-job failures into per-record skips with a report.

caseStudies.outcome: Peak runtime cut significantly; job performance metrics tracked run-over-run to drive iterative reliability improvements.

caseStudies.title: Redis-backed auth for client integrations
caseStudies.context: Amdocs: secure token services for onboarding new client integrations into the payment ecosystem.

decisions.problem: Token generation per request would hammer the identity provider
decisions.choice: Generate once per client (ID + secret), store in Redis for reuse, inject via x-authorization headers
decisions.why: Redis TTLs mirror token expiry so stale credentials evaporate on their own; a 35% storage optimization came from restructuring the token data model.

decisions.problem: Requests acting on resources they don't own
decisions.why: Ownership checks at the cache layer reject cross-tenant access before it reaches business logic.

decisions.problem: Partial failures in downstream token APIs
decisions.choice: POP token generator with configurable retries + circuit breaker
decisions.why: Fail fast when the provider is down instead of queueing doomed requests; custom exceptions distinguish retryable from terminal failures.

caseStudies.outcome: Improved authentication flow reliability across client integrations; reduced database load via the Redis session model.
